chunks.title: laplace formula sheet
chunks.text:
nabla ^2= partial _x^2+ partial _y^2+ partial _z^2

(TR) QCGreen 2) Core Properties Mean value property:

Value at a point equals the average over any sphere centered there.

No strict interior maxima or minima unless Phi is constant.

Dirichlet boundary data determine a unique solution.

In electrostatic equilibrium, Phi = constant inside a conductor.

Phi (x,y)=X(x)Y(y), qquad Phi (r, theta )=R(r) Theta ( theta )

(BL) QCOrange 3) Canonical Solutions Parallel plates:

Phi (x)=V_0 x d , qquad mathbf E=- V_0 d mathbf x

General spherical source-free radial solution:

Phi (x,y)= sum _ n=1 ^ infty A_n sin left ( n pi x a right ) sinh left ( n pi y a right )

(BR) QCPurple 4) Boundary Data and Checks Dirichlet:

Mixed conditions are possible on different boundary segments.

1. Verify nabla ^2 Phi =0 in the domain.

3. Check uniqueness before trusting an image or series solution.

4. In a bounded region, interior extrema usually signal an error.

or more carefully from the normal field just outside.

1. Image charges must lie outside the physical region.

2. Verify the boundary exactly, not approximately.

3. Use uniqueness before claiming the answer.

4. Grounded and isolated conductors are different problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
abstract This document is a structured manuscript scaffold for the exercises in Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics , 4th edition. The goal is to organize the textbook exercises by chapter inside the Astronomy & Astrophysics class format while keeping solutions out of the document. Problem numbers were indexed from the local book PDF and arranged chapter by chapter in a searchable LaTeX source. The output is a complete exercise index with placeholders for future paraphrased statements. This file is intended as a working template for notes, paraphrases, and later solution writeups.

This document intentionally does not reproduce the full textbook wording of the exercises. Each entry is a placeholder keyed by the original Griffiths problem number so that paraphrased statements or privately licensed transcriptions can be inserted later.

This chapter contains 64 indexed problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 1. 1 Using the geometric definitions of the dot product and cross product, prove that both operations are distributive. Do this first for three coplanar vectors and then for the general three-dimensional case. 1. 2 Decide whether the cross product is associative. Either prove that (( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C = mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) ) always holds, or give a simple counterexample showing that it does not. 1. 3 Find the angle between two body diagonals of a cube. 1. 4 Use a cross product to determine the Cartesian components of the unit vector normal to the shaded plane in Fig. ~1. 11. 1. 5 Prove the BAC--CAB identity by expanding both sides in Cartesian components. 1. 6 Show that ([ mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C)] + [ mathbf B times ( mathbf C times mathbf A)] + [ mathbf C times ( mathbf A times mathbf B)] = 0 ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Then determine the conditions under which ( mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) = ( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C ). 1. 7 For a source point at ((2,8,7) ) and a field point at ((4,6,8) ), compute the separation vector from source to field, its magnitude, and the corresponding unit vector. 1. 8 (a) Show that the two-dimensional rotation matrix preserves dot products. (b) Determine the conditions the entries of a three-dimensional rotation matrix must satisfy in order to preserve the length of every vector. 1. 9 Find the rotation matrix corresponding to a (120^ circ ) rotation about the axis through the origin and the point ((1,1,1) ), with the rotation taken clockwise when viewed along the axis toward the origin. 1. 10 Study how vector quantities transform under changes of coordinates. (a) Find how vector components change under a translation. (b) Find how they change under inversion ((x,y,z) mapsto (-x,-y,-z) ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: (c) Determine how a cross product transforms under inversion, decide whether the cross product of two pseudovectors is a vector or pseudovector, and name two classical-mechanics pseudovectors. (d) Determine how the scalar triple product transforms under inversion. 1. 11 Compute the gradient of each of the following scalar fields: (a) (x^2+y^3+z^4 ), (b) (x^2y^3z^4 ), and (c) (e^x sin y ln z ). 1. 12 The height of a hill is (h(x,y)=10(2xy-3x^2-4y^2-18x+28y+12) ), where (x ) and (y ) are measured in miles east and north of South Hadley, and (h ) is in feet. Find the hilltop location, the hilltop height, and the slope magnitude and direction at the point one mile east and one mile north of South Hadley. 1. 13 Let ( mathbf r ) be the separation vector from a fixed point ((x',y',z') ) to the point ((x,y,z) ), and let (r=| mathbf r| ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Show that (a) ( nabla (r^2)=2 mathbf r ), (b) ( nabla (1/r)=- mathbf r /r^2 ), and (c) derive the corresponding general formula for ( nabla (r^n) ). 1. 14 Let (f ) depend only on (y ) and (z ). Show that the gradient ( nabla f = ( partial f/ partial y) mathbf y +( partial f/ partial z) mathbf z ) transforms as a vector under a rotation in the (yz )-plane. 1. 15 Compute the divergence of each of the three vector fields ( mathbf v_a = x^2 mathbf x +3xz^2 mathbf y -2xz mathbf z ), ( mathbf v_b = xy mathbf x +2yz mathbf y +3zx mathbf z ), and ( mathbf v_c = y^2 mathbf x +(2xy+z^2) mathbf y +2yz mathbf z ). 1. 16 Sketch the vector field ( mathbf r /r^2 ) and compute its divergence. Explain why the result may seem surprising at first. 1. 17 Show, in two dimensions, that the divergence transforms as a scalar under rotations. 1. 18 Compute the curl of each of the three vector fields introduced in Problem 1. 15. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 19 Draw a clockwise tangential vector field around a circle in the (xy )-plane. By comparing nearby vectors, determine the signs of ( partial v_x/ partial y ) and ( partial v_y/ partial x ), use them to infer the direction of ( nabla times mathbf v ), and explain how this illustrates the geometric meaning of curl. 1. 20 Construct a nonconstant vector field whose divergence and curl both vanish everywhere. 1. 21 Prove product rules (i), (iv), and (v) from the list given in the text. 1. 22 (a) Write out the meaning of (( mathbf A cdot nabla ) mathbf B ) in components. (b) Compute (( mathbf r cdot nabla ) mathbf r ). (c) For the vector fields ( mathbf v_a ) and ( mathbf v_b ) from Problem 1. 15, evaluate (( mathbf v_a cdot nabla ) mathbf v_b ). 1. 23 Prove product rules (ii) and (vi), using the component definition of (( mathbf A cdot nabla ) mathbf B ). 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 24 Derive the three quotient rules corresponding to the vector-calculus product rules. 1. 25 For ( mathbf A = x mathbf x +2y mathbf y +3z mathbf z ) and ( mathbf B = 3y mathbf x -2x mathbf y ), verify product rules (iv), (ii), and (vi) by computing both sides explicitly. 1. 26 Compute the Laplacian of each of the following: (a) (T_a=x^2+2xy+3z+4 ), (b) (T_b= sin x sin y sin z ), (c) (T_c=e^ -5x sin 4y cos 3z ), and (d) the vector field ( mathbf v = x^2 mathbf x +3xz^2 mathbf y -2xz mathbf z ), interpreted componentwise. 1. 27 Prove that the divergence of any curl is identically zero, and check the result explicitly for the vector field ( mathbf v_a ) from Problem 1. 15. 1. 28 Prove that the curl of any gradient is identically zero, and verify the result for the function in part (b) of Problem 1. 11. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 29 Evaluate the line integral of ( mathbf v=x^2 mathbf x +2yz mathbf y +y^2 mathbf z ) from the origin to ((1,1,1) ) along three different paths, and then compute the closed-loop integral obtained by going out along one path and back along another. 1. 30 For the vector field used in Example 1. 7, compute the flux through the bottom face of the box, taking upward as positive. Decide whether the surface integral depends only on the boundary curve for this field, and then find the total outward flux through the full closed box. 1. 31 Compute the volume integral of (T=z^2 ) over the tetrahedron with vertices ((0,0,0) ), ((1,0,0) ), ((0,1,0) ), and ((0,0,1) ). 1. 32 Check the gradient theorem for (T=x^2+4xy+2yz^3 ) between ((0,0,0) ) and ((1,1,1) ) along the three paths shown in Fig. ~1. 28. 1. 33 Test the divergence theorem for ( mathbf v=(xy) mathbf x +(2yz) mathbf y +(3zx) mathbf z ) on the cube of side (2 ) shown in Fig. ~1. 30. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 34 Test Stokes' theorem for ( mathbf v=(xy) mathbf x +(2yz) mathbf y +(3zx) mathbf z ) on the triangular surface shown in Fig. ~1. 34. 1. 35 Using the same vector field and boundary line as in Example 1. 11, check Corollary 1 by integrating over the five faces of the open cube shown in Fig. ~1. 35. 1. 36 Prove the two integration-by-parts identities given in Eqs. ~(1. 60) and (1. 61). 1. 37 Invert the spherical-coordinate formulas and write (r ), ( theta ), and ( phi ) explicitly in terms of (x ), (y ), and (z ). 1. 38 Express the spherical unit vectors ( mathbf r ), ( theta ), and ( phi ) in terms of ( mathbf x ), ( mathbf y ), and ( mathbf z ), and then invert those relations to express the Cartesian unit vectors in terms of the spherical ones. Check the results using dot and cross products. 1.

chunks.text: 39 Check the divergence theorem on a sphere of radius (R ) centered at the origin for (a) ( mathbf v_1=r^2 mathbf r ) and (b) ( mathbf v_2= mathbf r /r^2 ). Compare the two cases and explain any apparent puzzle. 1. 40 For ( mathbf v=(r cos theta ) mathbf r +(r sin theta ) theta +(r sin theta cos phi ) phi ), compute the divergence and check the divergence theorem on the inverted hemispherical bowl of radius (R ) resting on the (xy )-plane. 1. 41 For (T=r( cos theta + sin theta cos phi ) ), compute the gradient and Laplacian, check the Laplacian by converting to Cartesian coordinates, and verify the gradient theorem along the path shown in Fig. ~1. 41 from ((0,0,0) ) to ((0,0,2) ). 1. 42 Express the cylindrical unit vectors ( mathbf s ), ( phi ), and ( mathbf z ) in terms of the Cartesian basis, and invert those formulas to write ( mathbf x ), ( mathbf y ), and ( mathbf z ) in terms of the cylindrical basis. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 43 For ( mathbf v=s(2+ sin ^2 phi ) mathbf s +s sin phi cos phi , phi +3z mathbf z ): (a) compute the divergence, (b) verify the divergence theorem on the quarter-cylinder shown in Fig. ~1. 43, and (c) compute the curl. 1. 44 Evaluate the following delta-function integrals: (a) ( int _2^6 (3x^2-2x-1) delta (x-3) ,dx ), (b) ( int _0^5 cos x , delta (x- pi ) ,dx ), (c) ( int _0^3 x^3 delta (x+1) ,dx ), and (d) ( int _ - infty ^ infty ln (x+3) delta (x+2) ,dx ). 1. 45 Evaluate the following one-dimensional delta-function integrals: (a) ( int _ -2 ^ 2 (2x+3) delta (3x) ,dx ), (b) ( int _ 0 ^ 2 (x^3+3x+2) delta (1-x) ,dx ), (c) ( int _ -1 ^ 1 9x^2 delta (3x+1) ,dx ), and (d) ( int _ - infty ^ a delta (x-b) ,dx ). 1. 46 (a) Show that (x ,d delta (x)/dx=- delta (x) ). (b) Let ( theta (x) ) be the step function. Show that (d theta /dx= delta (x) ). 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 47 Write the charge density in delta-function form for (a) a point charge (q ) located at ( mathbf r' ), (b) an electric dipole made of (-q ) at the origin and (+q ) at ( mathbf a ), and (c) a uniformly charged infinitesimally thin spherical shell of radius (R ) and total charge (Q ) centered at the origin. 1. 48 Evaluate the following three-dimensional delta-function integrals: (a) ( int (r^2+ mathbf r cdot mathbf a+a^2) delta ^3( mathbf r- mathbf a) ,d tau ), (b) ( int _V | mathbf r- mathbf b|^2 delta ^3(5 mathbf r) ,d tau ) over the cube of side (2 ) centered at the origin, (c) ( int _V [r^4+r^2( mathbf r cdot mathbf c)+c^4] delta ^3( mathbf r- mathbf c) ,d tau ) over a sphere of radius (6 ), and (d) ( int _V mathbf r cdot ( mathbf d- mathbf r) delta ^3( mathbf e- mathbf r) ,d tau ) over the specified shifted sphere. 1.

chunks.text: 49 Evaluate (J= int _V e^ -r , nabla cdot ( mathbf r /r^2) ,d tau ) over a sphere of radius (R ) centered at the origin, using two different methods as in Example 1. 16. 1. 50 (a) For ( mathbf F_1=x^2 mathbf z ) and ( mathbf F_2=x mathbf x +y mathbf y +z mathbf z ), compute the divergence and curl of each. Determine which can be written as a gradient field and which can be written as a curl field, and find corresponding scalar or vector potentials. (b) Show that ( mathbf F_3=yz mathbf x +zx mathbf y +xy mathbf z ) can be represented both ways, and find both potentials. 1. 51 For Theorem 1, prove the chain of implications ((d) Rightarrow (a) Rightarrow (c) Rightarrow (b) Rightarrow (c) Rightarrow (a) ). 1. 52 For Theorem 2, prove the chain of implications ((d) Rightarrow (a) Rightarrow (c) Rightarrow (b) Rightarrow (c) Rightarrow (a) ). 1. 53 Using the three vector fields from Problem 1.

chunks.text: 15, determine which can be written as gradients of scalar functions and find such scalars. Also determine which can be written as curls of vector potentials and construct appropriate vector potentials. 1. 54 Check the divergence theorem for ( mathbf v=r^2 cos theta , mathbf r +r^2 cos phi , theta -r^2 cos theta sin phi , phi ) on one octant of a sphere of radius (R ), making sure to include every piece of the boundary surface. 1. 55 Check Stokes' theorem for ( mathbf v=ay , mathbf x +bx , mathbf y ), where (a ) and (b ) are constants, around a circle of radius (R ) in the (xy )-plane centered at the origin. 1. 56 Compute the line integral of ( mathbf v=6 mathbf x +yz^2 mathbf y +(3y+z) mathbf z ) around the triangular path shown in Fig. ~1. 49, and verify the result independently with Stokes' theorem. 1.

(a) Combine the gradient theorem with Stokes' theorem for ( mathbf v= nabla T ). (b) Combine Stokes' theorem with the divergence theorem in the analogous way. 1.

63 For the field (r^n mathbf r ): (a) compute its divergence directly, test the result with the divergence theorem, decide whether a delta function is needed at the origin, and derive the general formula for ( nabla cdot (r^n mathbf r ) ); (b) compute its curl and verify the conclusion using Problem 1. 61(b). 1. 64 Define (D(r, epsilon )=-(1/4 pi ) nabla ^2[1/ r^2+ epsilon ^2 ] ). Show that as ( epsilon to 0 ), this quantity approaches ( delta ^3( mathbf r) ) by proving: (a) an explicit formula for (D(r, epsilon ) ), (b) (D(0, epsilon ) to infty ), (c) (D(r, epsilon ) to 0 ) for (r neq 0 ), and (d) the integral of (D(r, epsilon ) ) over all space is always (1 ).

This chapter contains 61 indexed problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 2. 1 Place twelve equal charges (q ) at the vertices of a regular 12-gon and a test charge (Q ) at the center. (a) Find the net force on (Q ). (b) Remove the charge at ``6 o'clock'' and find the new force on (Q ), explaining the symmetry argument carefully. (c) Repeat the central-force question for 13 equal charges on a regular 13-gon. (d) Remove one of those 13 charges and determine the resulting force on (Q ). 2. 2 Find the electric field at a point a height (z ) above the midpoint of two equal and opposite point charges ( pm q ) separated by a distance (d ). Give both magnitude and direction. 2. 3 A finite straight segment of length (L ) carries uniform line charge density ( lambda ). Find the electric field at a point a height (z ) above one end of the segment, and check that the result behaves sensibly in the limit (z gg L ). 2.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Check the limits (R to infty ) and (z gg R ). 2. 7 A spherical shell of radius (R ) carries uniform surface charge density ( sigma ). (d) Define the octopole moment and write the corresponding octopole term in the multipole expansion. 3. 53 Re-solve the problem of a conducting sphere in a uniform external electric field by the method of images, and verify that the result agrees with the solution previously obtained by separation of variables. 3. 54 In the infinite rectangular pipe, let the bottom (y=0 ) and the side walls (x= pm b ) be grounded, while the top (y=a ) is held at constant potential (V_0 ). Find the potential inside. Set up the separation in the style of Example 3. 4, include the (k=0 ) mode, and work out the full solution. 3.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 55 (a) A long square metal pipe of side (a ) is grounded on three sides, while the fourth side is insulated and kept at constant potential (V_0 ). Find the net charge per unit length induced on the opposite side. (b) Repeat the question for a circular pipe of radius (R ) split into four equal longitudinal conducting sections, three grounded and one held at (V_0 ); find the charge per unit length on the opposite quarter. 3. 56 An ideal electric dipole is fixed at the origin, pointing along the (z )-axis. A point charge is released from rest somewhere in the (xy )-plane. Show that its subsequent trajectory is a semicircle, like the motion of a pendulum whose support point is at the origin. 3. 57 A positive point charge (q ) of mass (m ) moves in a circular orbit of radius (s ) in the field of a fixed dipole ( p =p z ) at the origin.

Determine the orientation of the orbital plane and compute the speed, angular momentum, and total energy of the orbiting charge. 3. 58 Find the surface charge density ( sigma ( theta ) ) on a sphere of radius (R ) that reproduces, for all exterior points, the same field as a point charge (q ) located on the (z )-axis at position (a<R ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 4. 1 A hydrogen atom, with Bohr radius about 0. 5~ AA , sits between two parallel metal plates separated by (1 , mm ) and connected to a (500 , V ) source. Estimate what fraction of the atomic radius the induced displacement (d ) represents, and then estimate the voltage required to ionize the atom with that same apparatus. Use the polarizability listed in Table 4. 1. 4. 2 For a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the electron cloud has charge density ( rho (r)=q( pi a^3)^ -1 e^ -2r/a ), where (q ) is the electron charge and (a ) is the Bohr radius. Compute the atomic polarizability. A suggested route is to first determine the field of the electron cloud and then expand for (r ll a ). 4. 3 Equation 4. 1 says that an induced dipole moment is proportional to the applied field. Test that rule for a model atom whose electron-cloud density grows in proportion to distance from the center up to radius (R ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Find the attractive force between the charge and the atom. 4. 5 Two perfect dipoles, ( mathbf p_1 ) and ( mathbf p_2 ), are separated by distance (r ) in the geometry shown in Fig. ~4. 6. Find the torque on ( mathbf p_1 ) due to ( mathbf p_2 ), and the torque on ( mathbf p_2 ) due to ( mathbf p_1 ), each taken about that dipole's own center. 4. 6 A perfect dipole ( mathbf p ) is located a height (z ) above an infinite grounded conducting plane. The dipole makes angle ( theta ) with the normal to the plane. Find the torque on the dipole and determine the orientation of stable equilibrium if it is free to rotate. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 7 Show that the potential energy of an ideal dipole in an external electric field is (U=- mathbf p cdot mathbf E ). 4. 8 Derive the interaction energy of two dipoles separated by displacement ( mathbf r ), expressing the answer in terms of ( mathbf p_1 ), ( mathbf p_2 ), and ( mathbf r ). Use the dipole-energy formula together with the electric field of a dipole. 4. 9 A dipole ( mathbf p ) is located a distance (r ) from a point charge (q ), and ( mathbf p ) makes angle ( theta ) with the vector from (q ) to the dipole. Find (a) the force on the dipole and (b) the force on the point charge. 4. 10 A sphere of radius (R ) has polarization ( mathbf P( mathbf r)=k mathbf r ), where (k ) is constant. Compute the bound surface and volume charge densities, and then determine the electric field inside and outside the sphere. 4. 11 A short cylinder of radius (a ) and length (L ) carries a permanent uniform polarization parallel to its axis.

Find the electric field inside the cylinder, and show that the exterior field can be written in the form given in the text in terms of (a ), (s ), and ( mathbf P ). 4. 14 Show directly from the formulas for ( sigma _b ) and ( rho _b ) that the total bound charge of any polarized neutral dielectric is zero. 4. 15 A dielectric spherical shell with inner radius (a ) and outer radius (b ) has frozen-in polarization ( mathbf P( mathbf r)=(k/r) mathbf r ). There is no free charge.

A small cavity is carved out while the polarization remains frozen. Find the field and displacement at the center of (a) a spherical cavity, (b) a long needle-shaped cavity parallel to ( mathbf P ), and (c) a thin wafer-shaped cavity perpendicular to ( mathbf P ). Assume the cavity is small enough that ( mathbf P ), ( mathbf E_0 ), and ( mathbf D_0 ) are essentially uniform. 4. 17 For the bar electret of Problem 4. 11, with (L approx 2a ), draw careful field-line sketches for ( mathbf P ), ( mathbf E ), and ( mathbf D ).

The upper slab has dielectric constant (2 ), the lower slab has dielectric constant (1. 5 ), and the free surface charge densities on the plates are ( pm sigma ). Find ( mathbf D ), ( mathbf E ), and ( mathbf P ) in each slab, the potential difference, the full bound-charge distribution, and then recompute the fields from all charges as a check. 4. 19 You have just enough linear dielectric of dielectric constant ( epsilon _r ) to occupy half of a parallel-plate capacitor. Compare two arrangements: one where the dielectric fills half the plate area, and one where it fills half the plate separation. For each arrangement, determine the fractional change in capacitance.

Find the electrostatic potential at the center, measured relative to infinity, if the dielectric constant is ( epsilon _r ). 4. 21 A coaxial cable has inner conductor radius (a ) and outer conductor inner radius (c ). The annular region from (b ) to (c ) is filled with dielectric of constant ( epsilon _r ), while the region from (a ) to (b ) is empty. Sum the resulting series and compare with Eq. ~4. 49. 4. 24 An uncharged conducting sphere of radius (a ) is coated with an insulating spherical shell of dielectric constant ( epsilon _r ) out to radius (b ). The coated sphere is placed in a uniform external electric field ( mathbf E_0 ). Determine the electrostatic potential in all space. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Explain why the two answers differ and discuss which one, if either, should be interpreted as the physical energy of the system. 4. 28 Two long coaxial metal cylinders stand vertically in dielectric oil of susceptibility ( chi _e ) and mass density ( rho ). The inner cylinder is held at potential (V ), and the outer cylinder is grounded. Determine how high the oil rises between the cylinders. 4. 29 Return to the two-dipole arrangement of Problem 4. 5.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Compute the force on ( mathbf p_2 ) from ( mathbf p_1 ) and the force on ( mathbf p_1 ) from ( mathbf p_2 ), and comment on Newton's third law. A dipole ( mathbf p ) rides on the track and is constrained to remain tangent to the circle.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Use the dipole-force formula to show that the force is always forward along the track, and then explain why this does not create a perpetual-motion machine. 4. 32 Earnshaw's theorem forbids electrostatic trapping of a charged particle. Investigate whether a neutral but polarizable atom can be trapped instead. First show that ( mathbf F= frac 12 alpha nabla (E^2) ). Then show that (E^2 ) cannot have a local maximum in a charge-free region, so no stable electrostatic trap is possible. 4. 33 A dielectric cube of side (a ), centered at the origin, has frozen-in polarization ( mathbf P=k mathbf r ). Find all bound charges and verify that their total is zero. 4. 34 A parallel-plate capacitor is filled with dielectric whose dielectric constant varies linearly from (1 ) at the lower plate (x=0 ) to (2 ) at the upper plate (x=d ). The plates are connected to a battery of voltage (V ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Determine the complete bound-charge distribution and check that the net bound charge vanishes. 4. 35 A point charge (q ) is embedded at the center of a linear dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and susceptibility ( chi _e ). Find the electric field, the polarization, the bound volume charge density, and the bound surface charge density. Also determine the total bound charge on the surface and identify where the compensating negative bound charge is located. 4. 36 At the boundary between two linear dielectrics with no free surface charge, show that the field-line angles satisfy ( tan theta _2/ tan theta _1= epsilon _2/ epsilon _1 ). Then discuss whether a convex dielectric lens would tend to focus or defocus the electric field. 4. 37 A point dipole ( mathbf p ) is embedded at the center of a dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and dielectric constant ( epsilon _r ). Find the electrostatic potential both inside and outside the sphere. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 38 Prove the uniqueness theorem for a region containing a specified free-charge distribution together with linear dielectrics of known susceptibilities. Show that if the potential is specified on the boundary, then the potential everywhere inside is unique. Use the suggested integral involving ( nabla cdot (V_3 mathbf D_3) ). 4. 39 A conducting sphere at potential (V_0 ) is half buried in linear dielectric occupying the half-space (z<0 ). Test the claim that the potential is exactly the same as if the dielectric were absent. Do this by writing the candidate potential, finding the resulting field, polarization, bound charge, and free surface charge, showing that these reproduce the same potential, and then invoking the uniqueness theorem. Finally discuss whether the same idea works for the two modified geometries shown in Fig. ~4. 36. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 40 Use the force formula ( mathbf F= int ( mathbf P cdot nabla ) mathbf E_ ext ,d tau ) to find the force on a small dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and susceptibility ( chi _e ) located a distance (s ) from a thin wire carrying uniform line charge density ( lambda ). 4. 41 Relate microscopic polarizability ( alpha ) to macroscopic susceptibility ( chi _e ). Starting from the naive estimate (P=N alpha E ), account for the difference between the macroscopic field (E ) and the local field acting on a given atom, (E_ else ). Assuming each atom occupies a spherical cell of radius (R ), derive the Clausius-Mossotti formula connecting ( alpha ), (N ), and ( epsilon _r ). 4. 42 Check the Clausius-Mossotti relation numerically for the gases listed in Table 4. 1, using dielectric constants from Table 4. 2. Comment on why the correction term is hard to distinguish from the naive low-density approximation in gases. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

43 Derive the Langevin theory for a polar dielectric. First, using the Boltzmann distribution for dipole orientations in an external field, obtain the polarization law (P=Np[ cosh (pE/kT)/ sinh (pE/kT)-kT/(pE)] ) and sketch (P/(Np) ) as a function of (pE/kT ). Then take the weak-field, high-temperature limit to extract the susceptibility, and compare the prediction with data for liquid water at (20^ circ C ) and for water vapor at (100^ circ C ) and (1 , atm ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 5. 1 A charged particle enters a region where the magnetic field is uniform and directed into the page. From the observed upward deflection shown in Fig. ~5. 8, determine the sign of the charge. Then express the particle's momentum in terms of (a ), (d ), (B ), and (q ). 5. 2 For the crossed-field setup of Example 5. 2, find and sketch the particle trajectory when the initial velocity is (a) ((E/B) mathbf y ), (b) ((E/2B) mathbf y ), and (c) ((E/B)( mathbf y + mathbf z ) ). 5. 3 Recreate J. ~J. ~Thomson's charge-to-mass measurement for cathode rays. First use crossed electric and magnetic fields adjusted to give zero deflection to determine the particle speed in terms of (E ) and (B ). Then switch off the electric field, measure the radius of curvature (R ) in the magnetic field alone, and use it to obtain (q/m ). 5.

Then show that the polygon result approaches the circular-loop field as (n to infty ). 5. 9 Compute the magnetic field at point (P ) for each of the steady-current geometries shown in Fig. ~5. 23. 5. 10 Find the net force on (a) the square current loop near an infinite straight wire and (b) the triangular loop shown in Fig. ~5. 24, assuming all currents have magnitude (I ). 5. 11 Using the axial field of a circular loop from Example 5. 6, find the magnetic field on the axis of a tightly wound solenoid of radius (a ) with (n ) turns per unit length carrying current (I ).

Determine the field magnitude inside and outside such a solenoid, and show that the toroid field reduces to the solenoid result when the torus radius is very large. 5. 19 In Ampere's law, the enclosed current is written as (I_ enc = int _S mathbf J cdot d mathbf a ). Explain why this quantity is independent of which spanning surface (S ) is chosen for a given boundary loop. 5. 20 For copper, assuming each atom contributes one free electron, compute the density of mobile charges. Then find the average drift speed in a copper wire of diameter (1 , mm ) carrying (1 , A ), the magnetic attraction between two such wires (1 , cm ) apart, and the electric repulsion that would remain if the fixed positive charges were somehow removed. Compare the electric and magnetic forces. 5.

chunks.text: 21 Check whether Ampere's law is compatible with the vector identity that the divergence of any curl is zero. Show that Ampere's law cannot hold in general outside the magnetostatic regime, and discuss whether any analogous issue arises for the other three Maxwell equations. 5. 22 Suppose magnetic monopoles existed. Propose modified Maxwell equations and a corresponding force law that would incorporate them. If more than one consistent-looking option exists, list the possibilities and explain how experiment could distinguish among them. 5. 23 Find the magnetic vector potential of a finite straight wire segment carrying current (I ), taking the wire to lie on the (z )-axis from (z_1 ) to (z_2 ). Then check that your result reproduces the magnetic field formula for a straight segment obtained earlier. 5.

Then discuss whether this choice is unique. 5. 26 (a) Find the vector potential at distance (s ) from an infinite straight wire carrying current (I ), and verify both its divergence and its curl. (b) Find the vector potential inside the wire as well if the wire has radius (R ) and the current is uniformly distributed. 5. 27 Find the magnetic vector potential above and below the infinite plane current sheet discussed in Example 5. 8. 5.

Show, by applying Ampere's law around a path that loops around the wire, that the scalar potential cannot be single-valued. Then find the scalar potential for an infinite straight wire and explain why one must restrict attention to simply connected current-free regions. 5. 30 Use the rotating-shell result of Example 5. 11 to find the magnetic field inside a uniformly charged solid sphere of radius (R ) and charge density ( rho ) spinning with angular velocity ( omega ). 5. 31 Finish the proof that any divergenceless vector field can be written as the curl of a vector potential.

Determine whether the kinetic energy is conserved, write the force in cylindrical coordinates, derive the equations of motion, and describe the motion in the case ( dot z= ) constant. 5. 40 Parallel currents attract, so why does the current in a single wire not collapse onto the axis? Use angular momentum. 5. 44 Compute the magnetic force pulling together the northern and southern hemispheres of a spinning charged spherical shell. 5. 45 For a charged particle moving in the field of a hypothetical stationary magnetic monopole at the origin, derive the equation of motion, show that the speed is constant, prove that the quantity ( mathbf Q = m( mathbf r times mathbf v)-( mu _0 q_e q_m/4 pi ) mathbf r ) is conserved, show that the trajectory lies on a cone, determine the azimuthal evolution, obtain the differential equation for (r( phi ) ), and solve for the trajectory. 5.

Find the field (B(z) ) along the common axis for loops separated by distance (d ), show that the first derivative vanishes at the midpoint, choose (d ) so that the second derivative also vanishes there, and then find the field at the center. 5. 48 Use the axial loop field to derive the magnetic field on the axis of the rotating charged disk from Problem 5. 37(a), and show that the dipole approximation from the disk's magnetic moment is valid when (z gg R ). 5. 49 For a circular current loop, set up the Biot-Savart integrals for (B_x ), (B_y ), and (B_z ) at an off-axis point ((0,y,z) ), and evaluate (B_x ) explicitly.

chunks.text: 5. 50 Starting from the Biot-Savart law and the Lorentz force law for line currents, show that the force between two current loops can be rewritten in a form that makes Newton's third law manifest. 5. 51 For a plane current loop described in polar form by (r( theta ) ), show that the magnetic field at the origin is (B= mu _0 I 4 pi int d theta /r ). Check the result for a circular loop, use it for the lituus spiral closed by a straight segment, and then show that the field at the focus of a conic-section loop depends only on its semilatus rectum, not on its eccentricity. 5. 52 Use the analogy between ( mathbf A ) and ( mathbf B ) to derive a formula expressing ( mathbf A ) directly in terms of ( mathbf B ). Then derive the corresponding electrostatic formula expressing the scalar potential (V ) in terms of the electric field ( mathbf E ). 5.

Then use the alternative formulas given in the text to find ( mathbf A ) for uniform ( mathbf B ) and for an infinite straight wire, and comment on whether the result automatically satisfies ( nabla cdot mathbf A=0 ). 5. 54 Construct a magnetic scalar potential for (a) a pure magnetic dipole and (b) the spinning spherical shell of Example 5. 11. chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Derive a general integral formula for ( mathbf W ) in terms of ( mathbf B ), then use it for a uniform magnetic field and for the interior and exterior of an infinite solenoid. 5. 56 Prove the magnetostatic uniqueness theorem: if ( mathbf J ) is specified throughout a volume and either ( mathbf A ) or ( mathbf B ) is specified on the boundary, then the magnetic field inside is uniquely determined. Follow the vector-identity hint provided in the text. 5. 57 A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m=-m_0 mathbf z ) sits at the origin in a uniform external field ( mathbf B=B_0 mathbf z ). Show that there is a spherical surface through which no field lines pass, determine its radius, and sketch the field lines inside and outside that sphere. 5. 58 A thin uniformly charged torus rotates about its symmetry axis. Find the gyromagnetic ratio, then use the same idea to get the gyromagnetic ratio of a uniformly spinning sphere.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Finally, using the spin angular momentum ( hbar /2 ), estimate the magnetic dipole moment of the electron. 5. 59 Prove that the average magnetic field over a sphere of radius (R ), due to steady currents inside the sphere, is ( mathbf B_ ave = ( mu _0/4 pi )(2 mathbf m/R^3) ), where ( mathbf m ) is the total dipole moment of the enclosed current. Then show that the average field due to currents outside the sphere equals the field they produce at the center. 5. 60 A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius (R ) and total charge (Q ) spins with angular velocity ( omega ). Find its magnetic dipole moment, the average magnetic field inside, the far-field vector potential, the exact exterior vector potential, and the exact interior magnetic field. Check that the limiting results are consistent. 5. 61 Average the dipole magnetic field over a sphere centered on the dipole and compare the result with the general averaging theorem.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
Explain the mismatch and determine the delta-function correction needed in the exact dipole field at the origin. 5. 62 A thin glass rod of radius (R ) and length (L ) carries uniform surface charge density ( sigma ) and spins about its axis with angular speed ( omega ). Using a stack-of-dipoles picture, find the magnetic field at points in the (xy )-plane a distance (s gg R ) from the axis.

This chapter contains 29 indexed problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 6. 1 A square current loop and a circular current loop are arranged as in Fig. ~6. 6, with their separation (r ) much larger than the loop dimensions (a ) and (b ). Find the torque on the square loop due to the circular loop, and determine the stable equilibrium orientation if the square loop can rotate freely. 6. 2 Starting from the Lorentz force law in its line-current form, show that the torque on any steady current distribution placed in a uniform magnetic field is ( mathbf N = mathbf m times mathbf B ). 6. 3 For the two magnetic dipoles arranged as in Fig. ~6. 7, separated by distance (r ), find their force of attraction in two ways: first from Eq. ~6. 2 and then from Eq. ~6. 3. 6. 4 Derive Eq. ~6. 3 for the force on a magnetic dipole in a nonuniform field. Follow the suggested route of treating the dipole as a tiny square loop, expanding ( mathbf B ) in a Taylor series, and summing the forces on the four sides. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 5 A slab occupying (-a le x le a ) carries uniform current density ( mathbf J = J_0 mathbf z ). A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m = m_0 mathbf x ) is placed at the origin. Find the force on the dipole using Eq. ~6. 3, repeat the calculation for ( mathbf m = m_0 mathbf y ), and compare the expressions ( nabla ( mathbf p cdot mathbf E) ) and (( mathbf p cdot nabla ) mathbf E ) in electrostatics with their magnetic analogs by evaluating (( mathbf m cdot nabla ) mathbf B ) for the two cases. 6. 6 Classify the following materials as likely paramagnetic or diamagnetic: aluminum, copper, copper chloride, carbon, lead, nitrogen, salt, sodium, sulfur, and water. 6. 7 An infinitely long circular cylinder is uniformly magnetized parallel to its axis. Find the magnetic field produced by that magnetization inside and outside the cylinder. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Find the magnetic field due to ( mathbf M ) for points inside and outside the cylinder. 6. 9 A short circular cylinder of radius (a ) and length (L ) carries a frozen uniform magnetization parallel to its axis. Find the bound currents and sketch the magnetic field for the cases (L gg a ), (L ll a ), and (L sim a ). Compare the result with the bar electret problem from Chapter 4. 6. 10 An iron rod with square cross section, uniformly magnetized along its length, is bent into an almost closed ring with a narrow gap of width (w ). Assuming (w ll a ll L ), find the magnetic field at the center of the gap. 6. 11 The derivation of bound-current formulas starts from perfect dipoles, whereas real matter contains physical dipoles. Show, by the method used in Section 4. 2.

chunks.text: 3, that the bound-current treatment nevertheless gives the correct macroscopic magnetic field. 6. 12 An infinitely long cylinder of radius (R ) has frozen magnetization ( mathbf M = ks , mathbf z ), with no free current anywhere. Find the magnetic field inside and outside by two methods: first by locating the bound currents explicitly, and second by finding ( mathbf H ) from Ampere's law and then recovering ( mathbf B ). 6. 13 In a large magnetized medium the field is ( mathbf B_0 ), with ( mathbf H_0 = mathbf B_0/ mu _0 - mathbf M ), where ( mathbf M ) is frozen in. A small cavity is carved out. Find the field and ( mathbf H ) at the center of (a) a spherical cavity, (b) a long needle-shaped cavity parallel to ( mathbf M ), and (c) a thin wafer-shaped cavity perpendicular to ( mathbf M ). Assume all background quantities are essentially uniform over the cavity. 6. 14 For the bar magnet of Problem 6.

Use this approach and separation of variables to rederive the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere. 6. 16 A coaxial cable contains linear magnetic insulating material of susceptibility ( chi _m ) between two long cylindrical conductors. Current (I ) flows down the inner conductor and returns along the outer conductor, uniformly distributed over the surfaces. Find the magnetic field in the insulating region. Then compute the magnetization and bound currents and verify that, together with the free currents, they reproduce the same field. 6. 17 A long straight wire of radius (a ), made of linear magnetic material with susceptibility ( chi _m ), carries a uniformly distributed current (I ).

Find the magnetic field inside the sphere. 6. 19 Using the simple classical model of Section 6. 1. 3, estimate the magnetic susceptibility of a diamagnetic metal such as copper. Compare the estimate with the measured value and comment on the discrepancy. 6. 20 Describe a practical procedure for demagnetizing a permanent magnet and returning it to a state with zero magnetization when the applied current is zero. 6. 21 Show that the energy of a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is (U=- mathbf m cdot mathbf B ).

Treat the magnets as dipoles of mass (m_d ) and moment (m ). Find the equilibrium height of the upper magnet above a lower one placed back-to-back, and then determine the ratio of the two heights that occurs when a third magnet is added in the orientation shown. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Find the equilibrium separation, evaluate it for two electrons, and decide whether this implies a stable two-electron bound state. 6. 25 Use the dictionary ( mathbf D to mathbf B ), ( mathbf E to mathbf H ), ( mathbf P to mu _0 mathbf M ), and ( epsilon _0 to mu _0 ) to translate known electrostatic results into magnetostatics. Apply it to rederive (a) the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere, (b) the field inside a sphere of linear magnetic material placed in a uniform field, and (c) the average magnetic field over a sphere due to steady internal currents. 6. 26 Notice that uniformly charged, uniformly polarized, and uniformly magnetized bodies involve the same basic geometric integral.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Use this observation to derive the scalar potential of a uniformly polarized sphere and the vector potential of a uniformly magnetized sphere from the corresponding uniformly charged-sphere result. 6. 27 At the boundary between two linear magnetic materials, the magnetic field lines bend. Show that, in the absence of free surface current, the angles satisfy ( tan theta _2/ tan theta _1 = mu _2/ mu _1 ). 6. 28 A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m ) is embedded at the center of a sphere of radius (R ) and permeability ( mu ). Show that the magnetic field inside the sphere has the form stated in the text, including the added uniform term, and then determine the field outside the sphere. 6. 29 You are asked to referee a proposal aimed at deciding whether the magnetization of iron is better modeled by Ampere dipoles or Gilbert dipoles.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
The plan is to probe the interior field of a uniformly magnetized iron cylinder by carving a small cavity and placing a tiny compass needle inside. Decide whether such an experiment should be funded, and if so what cavity shape would be appropriate; if not, explain the flaw in the proposal.

7. 1 Two concentric conducting spherical shells of radii (a ) and (b ) are separated by weakly conducting material of conductivity ( sigma ). First show that ( partial mathbf B/ partial t= nabla times ( mathbf v times mathbf B) ) when ( sigma to infty ) and ( mathbf J ) stays finite. Then show that the corresponding generalized Lorentz force law is invariant under the same transformation.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 8. 1 Compute the power carried down the transmission lines of Example 7. 13 and Problem 7. 62, assuming the conductors are held at potential difference (V ) and carry current (I ) down one conductor and back along the other. 8. 2 Return to the charging capacitor model of Problem 7. 34. Find the electric and magnetic fields in the gap as functions of radius (s ) and time (t ). Then compute the electromagnetic energy density and Poynting vector, paying attention to the direction of energy flow, and verify the local conservation law. Finally, determine the total energy stored in the gap and show that the net Poynting flux into the gap equals the rate at which that energy increases. 8. 3 Use Maxwell's stress tensor to calculate the magnetic force of attraction between the northern and southern hemispheres of a uniformly charged spherical shell that spins with angular velocity ( omega ). 8.

chunks.text: 4 Consider two point charges separated by distance (2a ). Integrate Maxwell's stress tensor over the plane midway between them to find the force of one charge on the other when the charges are equal, and repeat when the charges have opposite signs. 8. 5 Two infinite parallel sheets carry surface charges (+ sigma ) and (- sigma ) and move side by side with speed (v ). Find the electromagnetic momentum in a region of area (A ). Then let the upper sheet move slowly downward until it reaches the lower one, and show that the impulse delivered during the collapse equals the momentum originally stored in the fields. 8. 6 A charged parallel-plate capacitor with uniform field ( mathbf E=E mathbf z ) is placed in a uniform magnetic field ( mathbf B=B mathbf x ). Find the electromagnetic momentum between the plates.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Then connect the plates with a resistive wire along the (z )-axis so the capacitor discharges slowly, and determine the total impulse delivered to the system. 8. 7 For an infinite parallel-plate capacitor with plate surface charges ( pm sigma ), compute all nine components of the Maxwell stress tensor in the region between the plates. Use the result to find the force per unit area on the upper plate and the momentum flux through a plane between the plates. Then imagine the supporting insulator is removed so this momentum flux is absorbed by the plates, and compare the resulting force with the earlier answer. 8. 8 In Example 8. 4, instead of reducing the solenoid current to turn off the magnetic field, connect a weakly conducting radial spoke between the cylinders so the electric field is discharged away.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Determine the total angular momentum transferred to the cylinders from the magnetic force on the spoke current, and compare it with the angular momentum originally stored in the fields. 8. 9 Two concentric spherical shells of radii (a ) and (b>a ) carry charges (+Q ) and (-Q ), respectively, and are immersed in a uniform magnetic field ( mathbf B=B_0 mathbf z ). Find the angular momentum stored in the fields. Then gradually switch off the magnetic field and compute the torque on each shell and the resulting angular momentum of the matter. 8. 10 An iron sphere of radius (R ) carries total charge (Q ) and a uniform magnetization ( mathbf M=M mathbf z ). First compute the angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields. Then study two different ways of releasing that angular momentum: gradual demagnetization of the sphere, and slow discharge through a grounding wire attached at the north pole.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: In each case, find the torque and the total angular momentum imparted to the sphere. 8. 11 Derive Eq. ~8. 39 for the upward magnetic force in the two-loop lifting model by treating the lower loop as a magnetic dipole. 8. 12 Derive Eq. ~8. 43 for the magnetic energy of two coupled current loops by building both currents up gradually from zero to their final values. 8. 13 A very long solenoid of radius (a ) and turn density (n ) carries current (I_s ). Coaxial with it, at radius (b gg a ), is a circular conducting ring of resistance (R ). When the solenoid current is slowly reduced, find the induced current in the ring. Then evaluate the Poynting vector just outside the solenoid and integrate it over the solenoid surface to confirm that it accounts for the power dissipated in the ring. 8.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 14 An infinitely long cylindrical tube of radius (a ) moves at constant speed (v ) along its axis and carries uniform line charge density ( lambda ) on its surface. A coaxial outer cylinder at radius (b ) moves with the same speed and carries charge density (- lambda ). Find the field energy per unit length, the field momentum per unit length, and the rate at which the fields transport energy across a plane perpendicular to the axis. 8. 15 A point charge (q ) sits at the center of a tightly wound toroidal coil of rectangular cross section, inner radius (a ), outer radius (a+w ), height (h ), and total current (I ). Assuming (w,h ll a ), find the electromagnetic momentum of the configuration. Then suppose the current is turned off quickly enough that the charge hardly moves, and show that the impulse delivered to the charge equals the initial field momentum. 8.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 16 A sphere of radius (R ) carries a uniform polarization ( mathbf P ) and a uniform magnetization ( mathbf M ), not necessarily parallel. Find the total electromagnetic momentum stored in the fields. 8. 17 Model the electron as a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius (R ) rotating with angular velocity ( omega ). Compute the total electromagnetic energy and total electromagnetic angular momentum. Then impose the assumptions (U_ em =m_ec^2 ) and (L_ em = hbar /2 ) to infer (R ), ( omega ), and the product ( omega R ), and assess whether the classical picture is self-consistent. 8. 18 Generalize the formulas for electromagnetic energy density, Poynting vector, momentum density, and Maxwell stress tensor to the case where magnetic charge and magnetic current are allowed. 8. 19 Consider an electric charge (q_e ) and a magnetic monopole (q_m ) separated by a distance (d ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Compute the total angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields. 8. 20 Show that an ideal stationary magnetic dipole ( mathbf m ) placed in a static electric field ( mathbf E ) carries electromagnetic field momentum ( mathbf p=- epsilon _0 mu _0( mathbf m times mathbf E) ). 8. 21 In Example 8. 4 the cylinders retain angular velocity after the solenoid current has been extinguished, so a residual magnetic field and residual field angular momentum remain. Compute that final field angular momentum in terms of the cylinder angular velocities, and determine the additional angular momentum transferred through the extra azimuthal electric field induced by the changing magnetic field of the rotating cylinders. 8. 22 A point charge (q ) lies a distance (a>R ) from the axis of an infinite solenoid of radius (R ), turn density (n ), and current (I ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
Find the linear momentum and the angular momentum, about the origin, stored in the electromagnetic fields. 8. 23 Rework the energy and momentum conservation arguments in matter using only the free charge and free current densities. Show that the Poynting vector becomes ( mathbf S= mathbf E times mathbf H ), derive the corresponding field-energy density, and then show that the electromagnetic momentum density takes the form ( mathbf g= mathbf D times mathbf B ). 8. 24 A circular disk of radius (R ) and mass (M ) carries (n ) identical point charges (q ) equally spaced around its rim. At (t=0 ) the disk lies in the (xy )-plane, spins with angular velocity ( omega _0 ), and is then released in the external magnetic field ( mathbf B(s,z)=k(-s , mathbf s +2z , mathbf z ) ).

Find the vertical motion (z(t) ) of the center and the angular velocity ( omega (t) ), describe the motion qualitatively, and confirm that the total kinetic energy remains constant.

This chapter contains 40 indexed problems.

chunks.title: README
chunks.text:
This repository is a graduate-level study of electrodynamics based on *Classical Electrodynamics by J. D. Jackson*, combining rigorous theoretical derivations with computational implementations and visualizations.

--- Website Live site: dajuarez4.github.io/Computational-Electrodynamics

Each topic follows a structured workflow: **Theory → Code → Visualization** Derivations written in LaTeX within Jupyter notebooks Numerical implementations using Python Visualizations to develop physical intuition

--- Repository Structure notebooks/ — Theory, derivations, and computational explorations cpp/ — Standalone solvers for larger numerical experiments and cross-language comparisons problems/ — Topic-based problem sets in LaTeX and PDF Jackson-problems/ — Chapter-based Jackson problem and solution sets Formula Sheet/ — One-page quad-chart formula sheets in LaTeX and PDF notes-diego/ — Personal study notes, chapter PDFs, and support materials repo-mindmap.md — High-level visual map of the repository

chunks.title: README
chunks.text:
--- Topics Covered Dirac delta functions Poisson and Laplace equations Green’s functions Method of images Multipole expansion Dielectrics Electromagnetic radiation Rectangular waveguide modes

--- Tools & Technologies Python (NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib) C++ (standalone FDTD solvers) Jupyter Notebook LaTeX (for mathematical derivations)

--- Topic Materials Dirac Delta : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Poisson's Equation : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Laplace's Equation : Notebook , Jackson 2.12 Notebook , Jackson 2.23 Notebook , Jackson 2.28 Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Green's Functions : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Method of Images : Notebook , Jackson 2.3 Notebook , Jackson 2.11 Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet , Chapter 2 Notes Multipole Expansion : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Dielectrics : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Electromagnetic Radiation : Notebook , Problem Set , Formula Sheet Rectangular Waveguide Modes : Notebook

The repository also includes interactive and static visualizations that build intuition for how the Dirac delta function behaves as a sampling device rather than as an ordinary spike. Mini Electrodynamics Lab

A browser-based electrostatics playground with draggable charges, field arrows, a potential heatmap, grounded-plane mode, and a test particle. Open the live lab .

Method of Images The animation below shows the grounded-sphere method of images: as the external source moves, the image charge changes position and magnitude so that the sphere remains at zero potential.

chunks.title: README
chunks.text:
Yee FDTD Method A 2D TMz Yee-grid simulation of a Gaussian pulse scattering from a dielectric disk. Open the notebook .

C++ Yee FDTD Solver A standalone 2D TMz Yee-grid solver that matches the notebook setup and writes CSV plus VTK output for direct comparison and later ParaView workflows. Open the solver . 3D Yee FDTD Solver A volumetric 3D Yee-grid solver with a dielectric sphere, Gaussian pulse source, and ParaView-ready .vti plus .pvd outputs. Open the 3D solver . 3D FFT Poisson Viewer An interactive 3D view of the periodic FFT Poisson solution, showing charge-density isosurfaces and equipotential structure exported from the C++ solver. Open the live viewer , Visualization notebook , C++ solver .

The goal of this project is to bridge **analytical electrodynamics** and **computational physics**, enabling a deeper understanding of physical phenomena through both theory and simulation.

--- Future Work Advanced numerical solvers for boundary value problems Interactive visualizations Time-dependent electromagnetic simulations Extensions to relativistic electrodynamics

This solver computes the electrostatic potential in a periodic 3D box by solving

$$ \nabla^2 \phi = -\rho / \varepsilon_0 $$

with a self-contained radix-2 FFT. What This Solver Does solves Poisson's equation on a periodic 3D domain writes ParaView-readable output as .vti supports two cases: gaussians for a physical positive/negative Gaussian charge distribution benchmark for an exact manufactured solution Quick Start

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
Dielectric media are central to electrodynamics because they provide the simplest nontrivial example of how matter responds to electromagnetic fields. Even in the static limit, the presence of polarizable material modifies charge distributions, boundary conditions, energy storage, and the relation between source charges and fields. The theory of dielectrics therefore occupies a foundational position between vacuum electrostatics and the more general electrodynamics of continuous media.

A dielectric is a material that contains charges bound to atoms, molecules, or more complicated microscopic structures, but lacks the freely mobile carriers that characterize a conductor. In electrostatics a conductor rearranges charge until the electric field in its interior vanishes, whereas a dielectric generally supports a nonzero internal field and responds instead by a displacement of positive and negative charge centers relative to one another.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
This displacement produces polarization. Microscopically one may imagine either the distortion of an already existing charge cloud or the reorientation of permanent molecular dipoles. Macroscopically the result is that the medium develops an electric dipole moment density. The induced charge associated with this rearrangement is called **bound charge**, in contrast with **free charge**, which denotes externally supplied or otherwise mobile charge that appears explicitly in macroscopic source descriptions.

The distinction is not merely terminological. If one writes Gauss's law directly in terms of the total charge density,

$$ \nabla\cdot \mathbf{E}=\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{tot}}}{\varepsilon_0} =\frac{\rho_f+\rho_b}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

then the bound contribution is entangled with the medium response. It is therefore natural to introduce new field variables, especially the polarization $\mathbf{P}$ and displacement $\mathbf{D}$, that encode the material response in a more systematic way.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The macroscopic polarization field $\mathbf{P}(\mathbf{r})$ is defined as electric dipole moment per unit volume. If a coarse-grained volume element $\Delta V$ contains many microscopic dipoles whose total moment is $\Delta \mathbf{p}$, then

$$ \mathbf{P}=\lim_{\Delta V\to \text{macroscopic small}}\frac{\Delta \mathbf{p}}{\Delta V}. $$

Its physical meaning is therefore direct: $\mathbf{P}$ points in the direction of positive charge displacement relative to negative charge displacement, and its magnitude measures the dipole moment density carried by the material.

To derive the effective charge density associated with a polarized medium, begin with the potential of a localized dipole distribution:

$$ \phi(\mathbf{r})=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{r}')\cdot \nabla'\frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }\,d^3r'. $$

$$ \mathbf{P}\cdot \nabla'\frac{1}{R} =\nabla'\cdot\left(\frac{\mathbf{P}}{R}\right)-\frac{\nabla'\cdot\mathbf{P}}{R}, \qquad R= \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' , $$

and applying the divergence theorem gives

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \phi(\mathbf{r})=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ \oint \frac{\mathbf{P}\cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}'}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }\,da' -\int \frac{\nabla'\cdot \mathbf{P}}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }\,d^3r' \right]. $$

Comparison with the Coulomb potential shows that the polarized medium behaves as though it carried the effective bound charge densities

$$ \sigma_b=\mathbf{P}\cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}. $$

These formulas admit a simple local interpretation. Consider a small pillbox-shaped volume element in a uniformly polarized material. Dipoles crossing the side walls cancel in pairs, while the faces normal to $\mathbf{P}$ acquire uncompensated charge. Spatial variation of $\mathbf{P}$ then produces incomplete cancellation from one volume element to the next, yielding the bound volume charge density $-\nabla\cdot\mathbf{P}$. The surface formula is the boundary analogue of the same counting argument.

Maxwell's electrostatic Gauss law for the total charge is

$$ \nabla\cdot\mathbf{E}=\frac{\rho_f+\rho_b}{\varepsilon_0}. $$

Substituting $\rho_b=-\nabla\cdot\mathbf{P}$ gives

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla\cdot\left(\varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{P}\right)=\rho_f. $$

This motivates the definition of the displacement field

$$ \mathbf{D}=\varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{P}, $$

The utility of $\mathbf{D}$ is therefore organizational: it separates externally specified or mobile charge from the induced polarization charge hidden in the medium response. In many boundary value problems the free charge is simpler to describe than the total charge, and $\mathbf{D}$ packages the complicated bound contribution into the constitutive properties of the material.

At the same time one must not overinterpret $\mathbf{D}$. Unlike $\mathbf{E}$, it is not a microscopic force field. It is a derived macroscopic quantity whose usefulness depends on the constitutive description of matter. In anisotropic, nonlinear, dispersive, or spatially nonlocal media, the relation between $\mathbf{D}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ may become substantially more complicated than the elementary form used in simple dielectric theory.

For a linear isotropic dielectric one assumes the constitutive relation

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \mathbf{P}=\varepsilon_0\chi_e \mathbf{E}, $$

where $\chi_e$ is the electric susceptibility. Then

$$ \mathbf{D}=\varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{P} =\varepsilon_0(1+\chi_e)\mathbf{E} =\varepsilon \mathbf{E}, $$

$$ \varepsilon=\varepsilon_0(1+\chi_e)=\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r. $$

Here $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity. The assumptions behind this form are substantial: **Linear** means the response is proportional to the applied field. **Isotropic** means no direction in the material is distinguished. **Homogeneous** means the constitutive parameters are independent of position.

Many real materials violate one or more of these assumptions. Crystals are often anisotropic, strong fields can produce nonlinear polarization, and composite materials may be strongly inhomogeneous. Nevertheless, the linear isotropic homogeneous model is the essential first approximation and underlies a large fraction of standard electrostatic applications.

For general dielectric media the equation $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{D}=\rho_f$ becomes

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla\cdot\left(\varepsilon_0\nabla(-\phi)+\mathbf{P}\right)=\rho_f. $$

If a constitutive relation $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon\mathbf{E}$ is available, then

$$ \nabla\cdot(\varepsilon \nabla \phi)=-\rho_f. $$

This is the generalized Poisson equation for an inhomogeneous dielectric.

When $\varepsilon$ is uniform, it reduces to

$$ \varepsilon \nabla^2\phi=-\rho_f, \qquad\text{or}\qquad \nabla^2\phi=-\frac{\rho_f}{\varepsilon}. $$

The physical interpretation is clear: the dielectric weakens the electric field generated by a given free-charge distribution relative to vacuum, because part of the total field is screened by polarization. In the inhomogeneous case the operator itself changes, and the spatial variation of $\varepsilon$ influences the field even where the free charge density vanishes. This is why dielectric interfaces require separate boundary conditions rather than a simple global rescaling of Coulomb's law.

Boundary Conditions at Dielectric Interfaces

Let an interface separate media 1 and 2, and let $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ point from medium 1 into medium 2.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
Applying Gauss's law for $\mathbf{D}$ to a thin pillbox straddling the interface gives

$$ \hat{\mathbf{n}}\cdot(\mathbf{D}_2-\mathbf{D}_1)=\sigma_f, $$

where $\sigma_f$ is the free surface charge density. Thus the normal component of $\mathbf{D}$ is discontinuous only if free surface charge is present.

Applying Faraday's law in electrostatics,

to a narrow rectangular loop crossing the interface yields

$$ \hat{\mathbf{n}}\times(\mathbf{E}_2-\mathbf{E}_1)=0. $$

Hence the tangential component of $\mathbf{E}$ is continuous across the interface.

For linear isotropic media this implies that the normal component of $\mathbf{E}$ is generally discontinuous when $\varepsilon$ changes, while the tangential component of $\mathbf{D}$ is generally discontinuous. These statements are simply different projections of the same boundary conditions once $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon\mathbf{E}$ is imposed. Physically, dielectric interfaces support polarization charge that alters the normal field while leaving the electrostatic tangential continuity intact.

The electrostatic energy density in matter is

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ u=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{E}\cdot\mathbf{D}. $$

This reduces to $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ in vacuum and to $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ in a linear isotropic dielectric. The presence of a dielectric therefore changes the amount of energy stored for a given electric field configuration.

For capacitor problems the consequence is immediate. If a dielectric fills the space between plates while the free charge is held fixed, the electric field decreases and so does the potential difference, changing the capacitance and the stored energy. If instead the voltage is held fixed by a battery, the free charge adjusts. Energy methods must therefore be handled with care: the correct thermodynamic interpretation depends on what external constraints are maintained.

Canonical Examples 1. Uniformly polarized slab

Take a slab with uniform polarization $\mathbf{P}=P_0\hat{\mathbf{z}}$. Then $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{P}=0$ in the bulk, so there is no bound volume charge. However, the faces normal to $\hat{\mathbf{z}}$ carry

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The slab therefore behaves like two oppositely charged sheets. 2. Uniformly polarized sphere

For a sphere with constant polarization $\mathbf{P}=P_0\hat{\mathbf{z}}$, again $\rho_b=0$ in the interior, but the surface carries

This produces a uniform internal field and an external dipole field. 3. Dielectric filling a parallel-plate capacitor

If a linear dielectric fills the gap between ideal large parallel plates carrying free surface charge densities $\pm \sigma_f$, then

$$ \mathbf{D}=\sigma_f \hat{\mathbf{n}}, \qquad \mathbf{E}=\frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon}\hat{\mathbf{n}}. $$

Compared with vacuum, the field is reduced by the factor $\varepsilon_r$. 4. Interface between two linear dielectrics

$$ \hat{\mathbf{n}}\cdot(\mathbf{D}_2-\mathbf{D}_1)=0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \varepsilon_1 E_{1n}=\varepsilon_2 E_{2n}. $$

The normal electric field therefore changes inversely with permittivity. 5. Point charge in a uniform dielectric medium

For uniform $\varepsilon$, Coulomb's law becomes

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \phi(\mathbf{r})=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q}{r}, \qquad \mathbf{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q}{r^2}\hat{\mathbf{r}}. $$

This is not a new law of electrostatics but the vacuum law combined with a simple constitutive response.

In all these examples the central theme is the same: the dielectric alters fields by generating bound charge through polarization.

The uniformly polarized sphere is one of the most important exactly solvable problems in electrostatics with matter. Let a sphere of radius $R$ have constant polarization

throughout the interior, while on the surface

$$ \sigma_b=\mathbf{P}\cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}=P_0\cos\theta. $$

The potential may be obtained by solving Laplace's equation inside and outside with this surface charge. The result is

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{in}}(r,\theta)=\frac{P_0}{3\varepsilon_0}r\cos\theta, $$

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{out}}(r,\theta)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{\mathbf{p}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{r}}}{r^2}, \qquad \mathbf{p}=\frac{4\pi R^3}{3}\mathbf{P}. $$

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
Hence the external field is exactly that of a point dipole at the center with dipole moment equal to the total dipole moment of the sphere. The interior field is uniform:

$$ \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{in}}=-\nabla\phi_{\mathrm{in}} =-\frac{P_0}{3\varepsilon_0}\hat{\mathbf{z}}. $$

This example is important because it links bound surface charge, multipole structure, and the macroscopic meaning of polarization in a clean and analytically exact way.

Now consider a dielectric sphere of radius $R$ and permittivity $\varepsilon$ placed in a uniform external field

The potential satisfies Laplace's equation both inside and outside. Axial symmetry implies that only the $\ell=1$ Legendre sector is needed:

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{out}}=-E_0 r\cos\theta+\frac{B\cos\theta}{r^2}. $$

Applying continuity of $\phi$ at $r=R$ and continuity of the normal component of $\mathbf{D}$ in the absence of free surface charge,

$$ \varepsilon \left.\frac{\partial \phi_{\mathrm{in}}}{\partial r}\right _{R} =\varepsilon_0 \left.\frac{\partial \phi_{\mathrm{out}}}{\partial r}\right _{R}, $$

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ A=\frac{3\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}E_0, $$

$$ B=E_0 R^3\frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}. $$

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{in}}= -\frac{3\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}E_0 r\cos\theta, $$

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{out}}= -E_0 r\cos\theta +E_0 R^3\frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}\frac{\cos\theta}{r^2}. $$

The second term outside is a dipole potential, so the induced dipole moment is

$$ \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{ind}} =4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^3 \frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}\mathbf{E}_0. $$

For weak susceptibility, $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_0(1+\chi_e)$ with $ \chi_e \ll 1$, the dipole moment is proportional to $\chi_e$. In the conductor limit $\varepsilon/\varepsilon_0\to\infty$, one obtains

$$ \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{ind}}\to 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^3 \mathbf{E}_0, $$

and the internal field tends to zero, as expected for a conducting sphere in electrostatic equilibrium.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: The macroscopic polarization field is a coarse-grained description. At the microscopic level matter contains discrete nuclei and electrons; the microscopic electric field fluctuates violently over atomic distances. Macroscopic electrodynamics smooths these variations by averaging over volumes large compared with microscopic scales but small compared with the spatial scale on which the external fields vary appreciably.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
This averaging justifies the use of continuous fields such as $\mathbf{P}$, $\mathbf{E}$, and $\mathbf{D}$. The relation between $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ can be motivated from molecular polarizability: an individual molecule may acquire an induced dipole moment $\mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{mol}}=\alpha \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{loc}}$. After averaging, one obtains a polarization density. However, the local field acting on a molecule is not generally identical to the macroscopic field, which is why microscopic derivations of constitutive relations require care. For present purposes the important point is that macroscopic dielectric theory is an averaged description, not a literal statement about pointwise microscopic charge motion.

Computational Demonstration: Bound Charge on a Polarized Sphere

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
For a uniformly polarized sphere with $\mathbf{P}=P_0\hat{\mathbf{z}}$, the surface bound charge density is $\sigma_b(\theta)=P_0\cos\theta$. The following code plots this distribution on the sphere and as a one-dimensional function of polar angle. It also compares the exact internal field with the theoretical value $-P_0/(3\varepsilon_0)\,\hat{\mathbf{z}}$ in normalized units.

The plot makes the angular structure of the bound surface charge explicit. Positive charge accumulates on the hemisphere toward which $\mathbf{P}$ points, negative charge on the opposite hemisphere, and the equator carries zero bound surface charge. The example is a concrete illustration that a uniform polarization field need not generate any bound volume charge, yet can still produce a nontrivial electric field through its surface charge.

Computational Demonstration: Dielectric Sphere in an External Field

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The dielectric sphere in a uniform external field is a classical boundary value problem. The code below evaluates the exact potential inside and outside the sphere, visualizes equipotentials and field lines in a meridional cross section, and extracts the induced dipole moment. We use normalized units with $\varepsilon_0=1$ and choose a moderate dielectric contrast so that the interior field reduction is visually clear.

The interior equipotentials are nearly parallel, reflecting the fact that the exact internal field is uniform. Outside the sphere, the field is the superposition of the applied uniform field and the induced dipole field. As the dielectric contrast increases, the field lines bulge more strongly around the sphere and the induced dipole moment approaches the conductor limit.

Computational Demonstration: Piecewise-Constant Permittivity in One Dimension

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\varepsilon(x)\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)=-\rho_f(x) $$

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
is especially transparent in one dimension. The code below solves a simple finite-difference problem with piecewise constant permittivity and localized free charge. It then plots the potential, electric field, and displacement field, allowing one to see directly how the dielectric interface alters the slope of the potential while preserving the expected interface conditions.

The potential remains continuous, as expected in electrostatics. The electric field generally changes across the interface because the permittivity changes, while the displacement field is much closer to continuity away from the localized free-charge region. This is the one-dimensional manifestation of the boundary condition $\hat{\mathbf{n}}\cdot(\mathbf{D}_2-\mathbf{D}_1)=\sigma_f$.

Bound charge is not an independent new species of charge. It is ordinary electric charge appearing in a reorganized form once the microscopic constituents of matter respond to an applied field. The split

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
is therefore a bookkeeping distinction within macroscopic theory, not a statement that nature contains two kinds of electric charge.

The constitutive relation $\mathbf{P}\propto \mathbf{E}$ breaks down when the response becomes nonlinear, anisotropic, hysteretic, frequency dependent, or nonlocal. Ferroelectrics, strongly inhomogeneous composites, and many optical materials lie beyond the scope of the elementary linear dielectric model.

Nevertheless, the simple theory developed here is foundational. It provides the conceptual bridge from vacuum electrostatics to electromagnetic waves in matter, refractive index, polarization currents, and the broader continuum theory of media.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: Key Takeaways Polarization $\mathbf{P}$ is electric dipole moment per unit volume. Bound charge arises from polarization according to $\rho_b=-\nabla\cdot\mathbf{P}$ and $\sigma_b=\mathbf{P}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}}$. The displacement field $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{P}$ satisfies $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{D}=\rho_f$. In a linear isotropic dielectric, $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon\mathbf{E}$ and the field equations are modified accordingly. Dielectric interfaces are governed by continuity of tangential $\mathbf{E}$ and the free-charge jump condition for normal $\mathbf{D}$. Dielectrics alter electrostatic energy storage and naturally enter capacitor and boundary value problems. Common Pitfalls Confusing free charge with total charge or treating bound charge as a separate fundamental charge species. Assuming $\mathbf{D}$ is more physically fundamental than $\mathbf{E}$ rather than a constitutive convenience. Forgetting that a uniform polarization can produce zero bound volume charge but nonzero bound surface charge. Applying $\mathbf{P}=\varepsilon_0\chi_e\mathbf{E}$ outside its regime of validity. Ignoring interface conditions when the permittivity changes spatially. Connection to Later Topics

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: The theory of dielectrics leads directly to boundary value problems in matter, capacitance and energy methods, forces in polarizable media, multipole descriptions of induced charge, and eventually electromagnetic waves in continuous media. It also provides the static foundation for later discussions of dispersion, susceptibility tensors, and more general constitutive response.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
This notebook develops the Dirac delta function at the level used in graduate electrodynamics, with the style and rigor associated with Jackson. The goal is twofold:

1. To understand the delta function as a mathematical distribution rather than an ordinary function. 2. To see why it is indispensable for compact descriptions of localized charge distributions in electrodynamics.

Throughout, we balance formal definitions with computational intuition using Gaussian approximations that become increasingly localized while preserving total weight.

In electrodynamics, one repeatedly encounters idealized sources that are sharply localized in space: a point charge, a thin charged wire, or an infinitesimally thin charged sheet. Physically, these are limiting descriptions of very concentrated charge distributions. Mathematically, ordinary functions are often inadequate for expressing such idealizations in a way that preserves the correct total charge.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
The Dirac delta function solves this problem. It is written suggestively as though it were a function, but its real meaning is operational: it extracts the value of a test function at a point. This makes it the natural language for describing localized sources in Maxwell's equations and in Poisson's equation.
